medications.indication_rationale: Simple analgesia for the tension headache; avoid aspirin/NSAIDs which impair platelet function.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1 g PO up to QID as needed (adult, no contraindication stated)

medications.indication_rationale: Antiplatelet effect increases bleeding risk in thrombocytopenia; avoid for the headache.
Not indicated at a safe count (~75); ITP treatment decisions are a haematology matter at much lower counts or with bleeding.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Log out of the pathology portal, do paced breathing, and stop researching platelet counts tonight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Removes the trigger and terminates the panic cycle.

patient_education_points.point: A platelet count of about 75 is a safe level — serious spontaneous bleeding, including a brain bleed, is very rare unless the count falls well below about 10-20. You have no bleeding signs and a normal headache pattern, so you are not bleeding into your brain.
Your headache is a tension/stress headache — use paracetamol (not aspirin or anti-inflammatories) and slow your breathing.

Go to ED or call 000 if you get a sudden 'worst ever' explosive headache, weakness or drooping on one side, slurred speech, lots of new tiny red spots or bruises, or bleeding that won't stop (nose, gums, urine, or stool).

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Repeat platelet count and haematology monitoring plan; health-anxiety support.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Thunderclap headache, focal neurology, new petechiae/bruising, or uncontrolled bleeding — ED/000.

must_include_items: Accurately reassure that ~75 platelets is a safe count (low spontaneous-bleeding risk)
Screen mucocutaneous bleeding and focal neurology/thunderclap headache before reassuring
Treat the tension headache with paracetamol (avoid aspirin/NSAIDs)
Provide a bleeding/neurological safety-net and routine haematology follow-up
errors_of_commission: Reflex ambulance/ED dispatch for a safe count with no bleeding/neurology
Telling her the count means she is at high risk of a brain bleed
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T1/T2 case with a bidirectional trap: passing requires BOTH accurate reassurance about the safe count/screen AND a bleeding/neurological safety-net. Over-triage or unscreened dismissal both fail.
